title: Devilled eggs
subtitle: Hard-boiled eggs with a mustardy, piquant filling
excerpt: Hard-boiled eggs halved and filled with a sharp, mustardy yolk mixture seasoned with mustard, vinegar, and cayenne. A British and American classic that makes an excellent starter or party dish.
sourceNotes: Stuffed and devilled eggs appear in British cookery from the Victorian period. Isabella Beeton includes stuffed egg preparations in Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The devilled version with mustard and cayenne follows the standard British devilling formula.

recipe.batchNotes: Scales easily. Make up to 24 halves for a party.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 4 hours ahead. Keep covered and refrigerated.

content.text: Devilled eggs are one of the most forgiving party dishes: they can be made ahead, they travel well, and almost everyone likes them. The filling should be creamy and rich with a clear background heat from the cayenne and sharpness from the mustard and a little vinegar. Pipe the filling for neatness, or use a teaspoon for a more casual result.

The eggs must be properly hard-boiled and cooled before peeling. An overcooked yolk will turn grey-green around the edge; a perfectly cooked one is bright yellow and just dry throughout. Ten minutes in boiling water, then straight into cold water, produces the right result.

Lower {{eggs}} eggs into boiling water and cook for exactly 10 minutes. Transfer immediately to iced or very cold water and leave for at least 10 minutes. Peel carefully. Halve each egg lengthways and pop the yolks into a small bowl.

Mash the yolks with a fork until smooth. Add {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, {{white-wine-vinegar}} of white wine vinegar, and cayenne pepper. Beat until very smooth and creamy. Taste and season with salt; the filling should be sharp, mustardy, and slightly spicy.

Spoon or pipe the filling back into the egg white halves. Dust with a little extra cayenne pepper. Garnish with chopped parsley if using. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before serving so the filling firms slightly.

items.fix: Cool immediately in cold water after exactly 10 minutes. The grey ring is caused by a reaction between sulphur in the white and iron in the yolk, accelerated by heat.

items.cause: Very fresh eggs have a thinner air pocket making the membrane adhere
items.fix: Use eggs that are at least 5 days old. The cold water soak also helps the shell lift cleanly.

items.cause: Yolks were not mashed finely enough before mixing
items.fix: Press the yolks through a sieve for an exceptionally smooth filling, or mash very thoroughly with a fork before adding the mayonnaise.

items.cause: Under-seasoned or insufficient mustard and cayenne
items.fix: Devilled eggs should have a clear, piquant flavour. Taste the filling before filling and increase the mustard, vinegar, or cayenne as needed.

content.text: Stuffed eggs with a spiced filling appear in British cookery from at least the Victorian period, when devilled preparations of all kinds were fashionable. The term devilled applied to the combination of mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce that characterised many small dishes of the era. In twentieth-century British and American cooking, devilled eggs became a fixture of buffets and party tables, where their combination of elegance, practicality, and universal appeal made them a reliable choice. They remain one of the most consistently eaten party dishes in both countries.
